f(x) is a function for which
                            A 3            A 3           A 1
                                C    D2
                                 f(x) dx +     f(x) dx =     f(x) dx
                              0                   0       0



     Which of the following claims about f(x) is/are necessarily true?

             I    f(x) ≤ 0 for some x with 1 ≤ x ≤ 3
                  B3               B1
             II   0
                       f(x) dx ≤   0
                                        f(x) dx

solution.rawText:
This question is about inequalities. To begin with, the only thing that we Rknow for certain
                                                                                          R 3 is that
       2                                                                      1
 f(x) ≥ 0 for all x, as it is the square of a real number. It follows that 0 f(x) dx ≥ 0 f(x) dx,
                                             R3               R1
as we are adding a non-negative number to 0 f(x) dx to get 0 f(x) dx. Therefore II is necessarily
true.
Let us now take II and rewrite the integral from 0 to 3 as the sum of the integrals from 0 to 1
and from 1 to 3; this is so that we can match the integral on the right hand side. We obtain:
                              Z 1           Z 3           Z 1
                                  f(x) dx +     f(x) dx ≤     f(x) dx
                                0              1            0

and we can now subtract the integral from 0 to 1 to get
                                       Z 3
                                           f(x) dx ≤ 0.
                                           1

Since the integral is negative or zero, the function itself must be so somewhere between 1 and 3;
if it were positive throughout the interval, the integral would be positive. Therefore condition I
must also be true.
Hence the correct answer is option D.
